I have received from you the rent for the 8th year -my half share of the 8 1/2 arouras in the vicinity of Polydeukeia - 3 1/2 artabas of corn. The artab tax has to be borne by you. Year 8, Epeiph 30.;Verso: Receipt for Saolis

title: Sale of a house
summary: This and P.Wisc. inv.66b (= P.Wisc. II 59) are copies of a contract of sale of a small building, formelly a mill. Titus Valerius Gaenus sells it to Aurelia Tapaeis and at the same time he cedes his rights to the products Aurelia Tapaeis may get out of the exploitation of the house. P.Mich. inv. 197 (= P.Mich. XII 627) records a later stage in the same transaction. It is reauest by Aurelia Tapaeis to the "bibliotheke egkteseon" of the Arsinoite noem to enter a provisional registartion to ensure the priority of her claims.
provenance: Ptolemais Euergetis, meris of Herakleides, Arsinoite nome, Egypt
translation_text: Year 14 of Imperator Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus and year 13 of Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pii Felices Augusti and year 6 of our lords Flavius Valerius Maximianus the most illustrius Caesars in the consulship of Ancius Faustus and Virius Gallus, Pharmouthi 10 in Ptolemais Euergetis of the Arsinoite nome. Aurelius Titus Valerius Gaeanus, son of Titus Valerius Satrianus, also called Serenus, and Iulia, Antinoite and however he is styled about 55 years old, with a scar --- acknowledges having sold to Aurelia Tapaeis, daughter of Papnuthis and Thaeisa, from the village of Philadelphia of the division of Herakleides, acting without a guardian in accordance with the customs of the Romans in virtue of the ius liberorum he, Titus Valerius Gaeanus, the party of the first part, to her in accordance with the agreement and through the property record office from now on for ever a house of one storey formerly a mill in which there is a dilapidated yard that belongs to him located in the same village of Philadelphia the house itself and what is around it with the measurements, cubits, foundations, walls, windows, entrances, exits of the same house and with all the appurtenances and all the rights as they are customary from of old up till now. The neighbours are, as they themselves dicated by mutual agreement, on the south and on the west ---, on the north the house of Arion, son of Sarapion, on the east the public street and he, the party of the first part, Titus Valerius Gaeanus --- has received from Tapaeis the entire price agreed upon, in full, three thousand silver drachmas, total 3.000 dr., forthwith from hand to hand; and both he, the party of the first part, and his representatives will guarantee to Tapaeis and her representatives the house sold to her as stated above with full guarantees and he will deliver the property unencumbered, unpledged, not offered as a collateral security, free from any debts, both public and private, and from any claims continually and he will see to it that no one hinders Tapaeis or her representatives from exercising ownership over the same and he, the party of first part, has not only sold the building but also the right to bring to her own place all that proceeds from it from now on and she has the right to exercise ownership and to manage it however she chooses on condition that neither he, the party of the first part, nor anyone else on his account will in no way whatsoever proceeds against it or claims it at his own costs and he will in addition forfeit the price increased by one half and the costs double and nevertheless the above stated will be valid. In answer to the question concerning this whether it is rightly and fairly done he has given his consent. (2nd hand) I, Titus Valerius Gaeanus acknowledge having sold to Aurelia Tapaeis the house that belongs to me in the village of Philadelphia in which there is a yard --- and I will deliver it free as above stated. (3rd hand) I, Aurelia Tapaeis and however I am styled, have received the deed of sale. I, Aurelius Heron, son of Aphiconius, have written it for her because she is illiterate. (4th hand) I, Aurelius Horion, senator and archivist of the land registry office of the Arsinoite nome through me, Aurelius Plution and however I am styled, have registered it as stated above.

title: Sale of a house
summary: This and P.Wisc. inv.66a (= P.Wisc. II 58) are copies of a contract of sale of a small building, formelly a mill (the present ppayrus has some divergences from the text of the other copy). Titus Valerius Gaenus sells it to Aurelia Tapaeis and at the same time he cedes his rights to the products Aurelia Tapaeis may get out of the exploitation of the house. P.Mich. inv. 197 (= P.Mich. XII 627) records a later stage in the same transaction. It is reauest by Aurelia Tapaeis to the "bibliotheke egkteseon" of the Arsinoite noem to enter a provisional registartion to ensure the priority of her claims.
provenance: Ptolemais Euergetis, meris of Herakleides, Arsinoite nome, Egypt
translation_text: Year 14 of Imperator Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus and year 13 of Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pii Felices Augusti and year 6 of our lords Flavius Valerius Maximianus the most illustrius Caesars in the consulship of Ancius Faustus and Virius Gallus, Pharmouthi 10 in Ptolemais Euergetis of the Arsinoite nome. Aurelius Titus Valerius Gaeanus, son of Titus Valerius Satrianus, also called Serenus, and Iulia, Antinoite and however he is styled about 55 years old, with a scar --- acknowledges having sold to Aurelia Tapaeis, daughter of Papnuthis and Thaeisa, from the village of Philadelphia of the division of Herakleides, acting without a guardian in accordance with the customs of the Romans in virtue of the ius liberorum he, Titus Valerius Gaeanus, the party of the first part, to her in accordance with the agreement and through the property record office from now on for ever a house of one storey formerly a mill in which there is a dilapidated yard that belongs to him located in the same village of Philadelphia the house itself and what is around it with the measurements, cubits, foundations, walls, windows, entrances, exits of the same house and with all the appurtenances and all the rights as they are customary from of old up till now. The neighbours are, as they themselves dicated by mutual agreement, on the south and on the west ---, on the north the house of Arion, son of Sarapion, on the east the public street and he, the party of the first part, Titus Valerius Gaeanus --- has received from Tapaeis the entire price agreed upon, in full, three thousand silver drachmas, total 3.000 dr., forthwith from hand to hand; and both he, the party of the first part, and his representatives will guarantee to Tapaeis and her representatives the house sold to her as stated above with full guarantees and he will deliver the property unencumbered, unpledged, not offered as a collateral security, free from any debts, both public and private, and from any claims continually and he will see to it that no one hinders Tapaeis or her representatives from exercising ownership over the same and he, the party of first part, has not only sold the building but also the right to bring to her own place all that proceeds from it from now on and she has the right to exercise ownership and to manage it however she chooses on condition that neither he, the party of the first part, nor anyone else on his account will in no way whatsoever proceeds against it or claims it at his own costs and he will in addition forfeit the price increased by one half and the costs double and nevertheless the above stated will be valid. In answer to the question concerning this whether it is rightly and fairly done he has given his consent. (2nd hand) I, Titus Valerius Gaeanus acknowledge having sold to Aurelia Tapaeis the house that belongs to me in the village of Philadelphia in which there is a yard --- and I will deliver it free as above stated. (3rd hand) I, Aurelia Tapaeis and however I am styled, have received the deed of sale. I, Aurelius Heron, son of Aphiconius, have written it for her because she is illiterate. (4th hand) I, Aurelius Horion, senator and archivist of the land registry office of the Arsinoite nome through me, Aurelius Plution and however I am styled, have registered it as stated above.

title: List of food
summary: On the third of Pharmouthi several items were distributed to 5 ifferent persons. We do not know why and by whom these products were given.
translation_text: Pharmouthi 3. To the (servant) of teh hypomnematographos: 16 eggs, 1 pig, 4 cocks, vegetables, 2 citrons; to Hegoumenos: 2 noney-cakes, 3 duck-eggs; to Phanias: fish, 4 duck-eggs, 2 citrons; to Ioustos: 1 jar, 30 eggs, 4 cocks; to the man from Anataeopoli: 1 jar.

title: Oath to a katasporeus
summary: An oath by a katasporeus stating that he has measured the entire plot of land sown. A list of the measured plots and what will be grown on them seems to follow (lines 16-22). As the katasporeus is also concerned with the sowing of land, one need not wonder that this task should be entrusted to him.
translation_text: Lines 1-12;In the consulship of our lords Imperator Diocletianus for the 8th time and Imperator Maximianus for the 7th time Augusti to Aurelius Horion, strategos of the Oxyrhynchite nome, from Aurelius Pausiris, son of Plutus, from the village of ---, katasporeus. I swear by the genius of our lords Imperatores Dicoletianus and Maximianus Augusti and of Constantius and Maximianus the most illustrious Caesares that I have measured everything of the cultivated area and that I have given the list of the receipts and expenses to ---;lines 24-31;Year 20 and year 19 of our lords Diocletianus and Maximianus Augusti and year 12 of our lords Constantius and Maximianus the most illustrious Caesares, Choiach 27. (2nd hand) I, Aurelius Pausiris, have sworn the oath as afore stated. I, Aurelius Horus, have written for him because he cannot write.

title: Order for commodities
summary: Paianios has placed an order with Alexander who is here reminded of the things he has to supply.
translation_text: [[To remind]] order of Paianios as a reminder for Alexander. 4 wooden beams, 2 sambatha wine, 4 ---, 800 almonds, 2 shepherd's crocks.

title: letter, receipr or an order for payment
Instruction for payment
summary: An ordinary instruction for payment of which many have come down to us. Phoibammon asks his "brother" Theodoros to give for his account 200 myriads of silver to a certain Olympios who is assistant to the "e commentariis"
translation_text: To my lord brother Theodoros, administrator, Phoibammon very good health and very many greetings. Give to Olympios, adiutor e commentariis for my account two hundred myriads of silver (denarii). total 200 myr. den. only. I pray for your health througth many years. Year 86 = 55, Mesore 16.

title: Instruction for payment
summary: This text has been published from a photograph by R. Remondon, L'Ȩglise dans la sociȨtȨ Ȩgyptienne �� l'eepoque byzantine CE 47 (1972), 254ff.  to illustrate the charity of a church. Several of these instructions given by the church have come doen to us (e.g. P.Oxy. VI 993; XVI 1950-1951).
translation_text: The holy church to Petros, oeconomos (of the churcg of) Kosmas. Give the widow Sophia from the coats which you have for good use one coat, total 1 coat only. (2nd hand) Greetings. (1st hand) Year 156 = 125, Mecheir 1 of the 3rd indiction.

title: receipt of money
summary: The papyrus belongs to the archive of the well-known family of Apiones. It is a receipt of money (1350 myr. of silver) as the price for 60 window-panes.
translation_text: recto: Paid by the most revenend rent-collector Apollos being the price of 60 window-panes which were bought for the use in the bath of the mansion outside the gate thirteen hundred and fifty myriads of silver, total 1350 myr. of silv. only (2nd hand) Total thirteen hundred and fifty myriads of silver only. (1st hand) Year 260 = 229, Epeiph 21 of the second indictio.;verso: (erd hand) Paid for the price of window-panes which were bought for the use in the bath 1350 myr. of silv.

title: Declaration of a shipper
summary: It is a formal acknowledgement by a shipper concerning the transport of a cargo to Alexandria.
translation_text: --- from the great ---- to Aurelius NN from the city of Oxyrhunchos, greetings. I acknowledge having received from you and having put on board [the aforesaid ship?] in the harbour of Oxyrhynchos --- fifteen the freight thereof being golden --- on condition that I will transport the cargo to the most illustrious (city of ) Alexandria and will deliver it to you unadulterated, pure and undamaged by any nautical damage --- and that I receive at the moment of the unloading from you the rst of talents (or nomismata). The contact is valid and in answer to the formal question I have given my consent. I, Aurelius Kollouthos, son of Pas, ----

title: Complaint from Anoup and Abraamios
summary: The language of the text is not all that lucid, yet it is possible to understand what the text is about. A certain woman Eirene has conducted an irrigation-channel to the land of Anoup and Abraamios with probably most unpleasant results. Anoup and Abraamios request the assistant of their (?) village to look into the matter and to decide if Eirene has the right to act as she told.;It is quite well possible that this papyrus belongs to the archive of the family of Apiones. We know that there was a conncetion between Tarouthinos and his family. The names Anoup and Abraamios are often found in documents pertaining to this family (though both names are common). In P.Oxy. XVI 2032, which belongs to the papers of this family, money is paid by an assistant Ioannes.
translation_text: From Anoup and Abraamios of Tarouthinos came accusing Eirene because she conducted a channel to their land though of old they need not allow this. Let your Excellency arrange it that each has his own land and if they have to allow that she conducts the channel (to their land) and nobody hindered her of old (then they will not object) but if at no time this channel was conducted (to their land) let it now also not be allowed to this woman.;verso: Deliver to his Excellency Ioannes, assistant of TArouthinos from Anoup and Abraamios

title: Diotimos
summary: The chief interest of this private letter is to be found that it provides us with an exactly dated letter from the first century A.D. Aphrodite who is staying with a certain Diotimos receives an allowance from her family. This allowance is paid six times. Diotimos reports that he received Aphrodite's allowance up till Pharmouthi. It is probably a coincidence that Choiach is the end of the first period of the Egyptian year and Pharmouthi the end of the second period. As this letter was written in Hathyr and it is unlikely that the payments were made in advance, this papyrus should in all probability be dated November 17, A.D. 94.
provenance: Theadelphia or Philadelphia, villages of the Arsinoite nome, Egypt (?)
translation_text: Diotimos to Isarous greetings. I have recived from Aigialos as a first allowance for Aphrodite 22 dr. and as a second allowance 24 dr. also from Aigialos, as a third allowance 24 dr. and as a fifth allowance you have sent 12 dr. of wool and I have received from Aigialos the remaining 12 dr. so that I have the fifth allowance up to Choiach of the 13th year of Domitianus the lord and you have sent me through Heraklas a chiton worth 24 dr. so that I have it up to Pharmouthi. Greet your children and your husband. Aphrodite greets all of you. Greet your mother and all your people. Farewell. Hathyr 21.;verso: To Theadelphia to Isarous concerning the allowance of Aphrodite.

title: Antonis
summary: The chief interest of this letter lies in the fact that it is dated and supplies us with an example of the kind of handwriting people might use and how they might misspell words about A.D. 100.;Antonis writes to his mother and his mother in-law or his father (see n. 9 of the edition) to send him some blankets, because he and his wife (?) Themouthas are freezing. He is prepared to pay them and he brings in his wife (?) to reinforce his request.
provenance: Philadelphia, villages of the Arsinoite nome, Egypt (?)
translation_text: Antonis to his mother Valerias greetings. Being asked about the blankets I am writing to you send them to me through Valerius and you will tell him the price because it is winter and because of Thermouthas because we need them and I myself do not have them and I have written to my mother for another pair as i wrote to you so I wrote to her. Do not otherwise, mother, because nobody will blame you on account of the expenses. Thermouthas sends her greetings to you and Valeras and her father and Valerius and Dionysia and demetria and all persons of your household. Year 4 of Imperator Nerva Traianus Augustus, Germanicus 7.

title: Letter to the decurion Teres
summary: A senior officer (higher in rank than a centurion, prsesumably a praefectus castrorum) informs the decurion Teres that at the request of the strategos of the Coptite nome he is granted thirty day's leave to look after his posesiones in the Arsinoite jome. It is not clear to us why Teres asked for this leave. One immediately thinks of a connection with the aftermath of the Jewish revolt under the reign of Trajan, but another possibility is that Teres, in active services, possesed property in the Arsinoite nome which he had let or hire. For another request of leave cf. P.I Price, some Roman ostraca from Egypt, JJP 9-10 (1955-1956), no 2 (SB VI 9272). Cf. also P.Mich. XII 629 and O.Flor. 1. For the dots between thw words see E. Otha Wingo, Latin Punctuation in the classical Age, The Hague-Paris 1972. It is not necessary that this papyrus was found in the Coptite nome.;If the strategos is named Chrysermus, then the text should be dated in c. A.D. 103 (BL IX, 374)
translation_text: [NN] to the decurion Teres, greetings. [ ]stermus, strategos of the Coptite nome has asked me to grand you 30 days' leave so that you may visit your possessions which you have in the Arsinoite nome. I have written not only to him but also to the centurion Petronius Fidus that I grant this ----

title: Letter from Ptolemaios to Kassianos Gemellos
summary: Ptolemaios has received a letter from his brother Kassianos Gemellos  (Cassianus Gemellus) informing him that his brother had been in the neighbouhood. He more or less blames Kassianos for not coming up-country and paying him a visit. Ptolemaios urges Kassianos not to fail to visit him next time he comes his brother's way. He promises to send a sum of money to Kassianos. If he cannot find someone to take it to Kassianos, Ptolemaios will pay Longinus back in the place where he lives, situated south of Karanis.
provenance: Karanis, Herakleidou meris, Arsinoite nome, Egypt
translation_text: Ptolemaios to Kassianos, his brother, very many greetings. Before anything else I pary for your health. I have received one keramion --- and a letter in which you write that you had come ---. At the same time you could have come up-country to us so that we too --- because it is sweet to greet you in person. If he wants you to come in good health another time I implore you to come up-country to me. If I find someone who can bring you Proklous (Proclus)'s money I will send it through him, if not, if Longinos (Longinus) has not departed, you will speak to him with my words and you will take it from him and write to me through him and I will give him here hundred and twenty-four drachmas. Greet Sarapias and her mother and Baukalas and all your people, each by name. I pray for your health, lord. Mecheir 17.

title: Letter from Kaikilios Gemellos (Caecilius Gemellus) to Didymarion
summary: Kaikilios Gemellos (Caecilius Gemellus), a soldier, writes to his sister, who is probably his wife that he has safely arrived at the barracks. As the papyrus is damaged along the folds by two large holes, it is no longer possible to ascertain what he wrote in lines 11-16. He says something about a journey downstream, about a certain Sambathius (or Sambathion) (lines 13-15) and about Didymarion's son (lines 16-17). He continues by asking Didymarion to send him some olives. The letter ends with the usual greeting formulae. ;On the evsro there are traces of 2 lines of the address which are too faint to be read.
translation_text: Kaikillis Gemellos (Caecilius Gemellus) to Didymarion his sister and lady, very many greetings. Before anything else I pray for your good health and that of your children - may the evil eye not touch them -. I want you to know, sister, that I arrived (safely) at the barracks on the 20th of Pharmouthi. On the way Nepotilla met me and informed me that you did not hear about Sambathion. Your son behaved badly in not informing you at New Year about what he heard when he was here. Send me please, sister, when you have enough olives, (some of them) through your son, if he comes, if not buy them for me at the current price. I ask you, sister, write me quickly about your welfare and that of your children.Greet all your people and all the persons of your household. I pray for your health.

title: Letter from Didymos to Hephaistion
summary: The most important feature of this letter is that it mentions a new strategos of the Oxyrhunchite nome. Philonikos, to whom the addressee of thius letter is secretary, was unknown. The person who delivered this letter was probably Theon (lines 14/14 and 22).
translation_text: Didymos to Hephaistion my lord brother greetings. As our very good brother Phabanon writes to you we very much rejoiced and had a party when we reveived your letter. All our people are well and even more so after they received your letter. Therefore do it without delay knowing that this is our expectation. Phabanon wrote to you about everything also about the mixed produce. Nothing has been done about that because Theon will tell you in person how cheap it is. Hurry quickly and come to us as you wrote. All the persons in the house greet you. Buy a tunic for Thermuthis, she has nothing to put on. Receive the suit from theon the most honoured. I pray for your good health, worthy lord.;To Hephaistion, secretary of Philonikos, strategos of the Oxyrhynchite nome.

title: Letter from Kyra and Aia
translation_text: Cyra and Aia to their lord and brother Apynchios, very many greetings. Put all things aside and come immediately to us because our mother has died and we badly need your presence. Therefore, please, do not stay at your place but come to us or we shall ruin our household. You yourself know that we cannot do anything (without you. Eagerly we wait) your presence. For that reason we have also sent our brother Martyrios so that you may come immediately to us with him. We must be very grateful to him because he undertook the trouble of the journey to you although we paid the expenses. We greet our sister, your wife, and her children -may the evil eye not touch them - and through many years. Do not neglect it and feel repentance afterwards. Come immediately to us so that you may arrange everything.;verso: Deliver to our lord and brother Aphynchios from Kyra and Aia.

title: Letter from Dioskoros to Ammonianos
summary: Dioskoros askes his brother Ammonianos to sent him an assistant for a short time. As the letetr is damaged at the beginning of line 6, we do not know why Amois was needed.
translation_text: To my lord Ammonianos Dioskoros, greetings. the assistant Amois is needed for the season of ---. Order him, therefore, to come to me in Pompanos and I will send him back to you in no time. Farewell through many years my lord brother.;verso: To my lord brother.

title: Letter from Dioskoros to Ammonianos
summary: The letter is definitely Christian (cf. lines 3 and 27; also 34-35). The role of Didymos and the people of Apis (line 12) is obscure. Neither is it clear what to make of the two mattresses in line 16. There may be a connection with "upaithrios" in l. 15. The document on the other side is dated to A.D. 303, but our letter on the verso is of a later date.
translation_text: Taarpaesis;;Tausiris;;Camilla;;Didymos;;Apis

title: Account
summary: Payments to labourers
provenance: Philadelphia, Herakleidou meris, Arsinoite nome, Egypt
translation_text: To Labois for those cleaning up beforehand and burning the bitter land in the 2nd enclosure, the one to the south of the croton field, for 20 labourers, at _ of an ob., 2 dr., 3 od., and for those in the 3rd enclosure, the one to the south, for 20 labourers, 3 dr., 2 ob. And to Onnophris for those picking sesame in the 1st enclosure, the one toward the south, for 10 labourers, at _ of an ob., 1 dr., 1 _ ob. And to Pasis for those preparing the small supply-ditches in the flax field, for 20 labourers, 3 dr., 2 ob.. And to Kerkion for hoers, those (working) in the 2nd enclosure because the soil was cracked and could not be ploughed, for 20, 3 dr., 2 ob., and for boys, those cleaning up beforehand and burning the bitter land, 30 of them, at _ an ob., 2 dr., 3 ob. Total for the day 36 dr., 4 ob. 22nd. To Labois for those cleaning up beforehand and burning the bitter land in the 2nd enclosure, the one in the south of the croton field, for 20 labourers, at _ of an ob., 2 dr., 3 ob., and for those in the 3rd enclosure, the one to the south of the croton field, for 20 labourers, 3 dr., 2 ob. And to Onnophris for those picking sesame in the 1st enclosure, the one toward the south, for 10 labourers, at _ of an ob., 1 dr., 1 _ ob. And to Pasis for those preparing the small supply-ditches in the flax field, for 20 labourers, 3 dr., 2 ob. And to Kerkion for hoers, those (working) in the 2nd enclosure because the soil was cracked and could not be ploughed, for 20, 3 dr., 2 ob., and for boys, those cleaning up beforehand and burning the bitter land, 30 of them, at _ an ob., 2 dr., 3 ob. Total 16 dr., 1 _ ob. 23rd. For those working at the supply-ditch in the 2nd enclosure so as to lead the water into the 3rd in order to irrigate the high ground, to Atmeus, son of Petesis, of Aphroditopolis, for 300 aioilia, 20 dr. And to NN for 20 labourers engaged in banking up ---;Verso: Year 31 from the bank of Artemidoros from Thoth to Tybi 30th. Mesore 5th.

title: Account
summary: The Wisconsin part of this papyrus (lines 33-40; 74-79; 108-113; 172-180) was originally published by G. Malz, Another Zenon Papyrus at the University of Wisconsin, AJA 39 (1935), pp. 373-376 (= SB V 7686).;At least one line has been lost between lines 32 and 33 (there may have also been a blank space of one line preceding the account for Tybi). More than one line may have been lost between lines 72 and 74 (the upper edge of column II of P.Wisc. inv. 2 is blank). One or two lines and a blank space must have been lost between lines 107 and 108, while between lines 171 and 172 again a few lines seem to be missing. ;Lines 112 and 113 seem to be written by a different hand. For the hands on the verso cf. PCZ III 59328, 116-160 note and G. Malz, loc. cit.
translation_text: P.Wisc. inv. 2:;On the 1st of Tybi 1 for Athenagoras 1 (kid), 4 cheeses; on the 2nd for Athenagoras 1, 4 cheeses; on the 3rd for Athenagoras 1, 4 cheeses; on the 4th for Athenagoras 1, 4 cheeses; on the 5th for Athenagoras 1, 4 cheeses; These have been given through Limnaios;;For Kallippos, on receipt of a letter, a kid which the brother Limnaios received; on the 15th for Kritolaos through Antipatros 1 kid;;of which was spent for wages (for the care) of the offspring of the 140 (goats) for Epeiph and Mesore of the 36th year, each month, 3 drachmae, 3 obols, total 7 drachmae.;162;30;;verso:;of these for Sostratos 2kids, Nikandros has the remainder 212, of these 15 for Asklepiades, 5 for Philinos 5 He has 1, for sale 192 and what he has deducted on this he gave to Philinos 4;Total 196;

title: Penthemeros-certificate
translation_text: The eleventh year of the emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus. Has worked for 5 days in fulfillment of his obligations of the same 11th year for work on the embankments in the new six-gate sluice Payni 29 --- to Epeiph 3 on behalf of Karanis, Krates, son of Sokrates and Thaesis, grandson of Myshthes. I, Isidoros, have signed it.

title: Customs account
summary: The Wisconsin part of this papyrus, immediately following P.Merton I 15 was originally published by NY Clauson, A Customs House Registry from Roman Egypt (P.Wisconsin 16), Aegyptus 9 (1928), 240 ff (= SB IV 7365).

Tot. for the day, 4 dr., 3 ob. 15th, Horion, vinegar, 2 jars at 1 dr., 12 ob. per jar, tot. 2 dr., 3 ob. title: Sentence of the praefect Valerius Eudaemon
translation_text: Year six of Antoninus Caesar the lord, the sevententh Mecheir. With Kallinikos, former village secretary, in attendance. And Kallinikos having approached the tribunal, after other matters Eudaimon said to Kallinikos: "What did you have in mind when you put forward an indigent man for liturgy? Being the cause of his flight, you (thereby) were the cause that his possessions were sold. You are subject to mpenalties. In the treasury office you will pay the fines, but, also, to him four times as much as that for which his property was sold.

title: Two letters from Sempronius
translation_text: Sempronius to his brother Satornilos greetings. I have received two letters from you, one concerning the things I have made clear to our brother Maximos, the other about our lady mother, that she has been in danger and that she is still unwell. You must know, brother, that I shall be very anxious and that at the same time I shall not sleep at night until you let me know how is doing in that climate. Do not waste time until you find someone who will sail downstream to me. Confort our brother Maximos as well as you can. I have also written to our brother Valerios about that. I hope that he also realizes how deeply sorry we feel about [ ]; but how can we be of any help thereby? Not he alone but all of us are bereft of her. Therefore, regard us as not being able to help, but do not think humanly in excess certainly not such a moment.;;Sempronius to his mother Satornila, greetings. the moment you receive this letters let me know how are doing because I am not a littele worried until I hear about your affairs. Concerning my brother Maximos I will now write to you so that you may comfort him --- the unsuitable times do not leave me --- that not he, too, out of sorrow turns himself towards something else. For I know that you, too, are in sorrow on behalf of him, but, lady, come to your senses because of my brothers. And this had to be because he loved his daughters and his (?) sister Siborra equally. But can we do against a thing against which God cannot do anuthing? Farewell.;;verso: deliver to my brother Valerius and to my brother satarnilos from Sempronios.

title: Liturgy list
summary: If N. Lewis (op. cit., introduction to text no. 2) is correct in his assumption that this papyrus should be dated to the latter part of the IInd century A.D., year 6 mentioned in line 28 could be the sixth year of the reign of Septimius Severus (15.10.197 A.D.). However, the sixth year of the reign of Alexander Severus (15.10.226 A.D.) cannot be excluded. The text is a nomination of every time two men to superintend the irriügation of the fields. First two men of each six toparchies of the Oxyrhynchite nome are nominated (lines 6 - 27); from line 29 onwards at least 15 more men are nominated. These men are living in certain villages of the Oxyrhynchite nome. It is possible that each village contributed two men as well (it is not absolutely certain that line 37 was directly followed by line 38 = the beginning of the second London fragment). Anyhow, one village must have contributed either one or three nominees. The text is in all probability a draft. The addressee is not named; the text is written on the back of another document (our text no. 49), and the entries are all different and far from complete. I have in most instances refrained from resolving the abbreviated proüper names when there was more than one possibility.

title: Petition to dekaprotoi
provenance: Philadelphia, Herakleidou meris, Arsinoite nome, Egypt
translation_text: To the dekaprotoi of the 2nd toparchy of the division of Herakleides from (2nd hand) Valerius Diogenes, Valerius Longinus and Valerius Clementinus, all sons of Lucretius, Antinoites. We own in the vicinity of the village of Philadelphia, in sect. 4 eight and a half arouras of grain-producing land under seed, in sect. 5 four and a half arouras of unwatered land, in sect. 7 seven arouras of unwatered land, and ten orchard arouras partly dead, in sect. 14 likewise seven orchard arouras, three of which have been cut over, in Sect. 20 six and a half orchard arouras and two totally cut over arouras. But the village registrar, possibly through error, assessed us in the posted list of individual taxpayers with the following: in Sect. 4 a total of nine and five-eighths arouras under seed, adding an aroura and an eighth, in Sect. 5 a total of six arouras of unwatered land, adding an aroura and a half, in Sect. 7 a total of 15 1/2 arouras of unwatered land and eleven and a half orüchard arouras, adding eight and a half arouras of unwatered land and one and a half orchard arouras, in Sect. 14 in our names and in that of our maternal grandmother, Harsous, a total of ten and seventeen thirty-seconds orchard arouras, adding three and seventeen thirty-seconds arouras, in Sect. 20 in the same names twenty-three and a half orchard arouras, adding fifteen arouüras, and subtracting three and a half arouras from the seven arouras of unüwatered grain-producing land which we have in the same section. Wherefore, the wrong being not slight, we submit this petition, attesting in accordance with the orders given by Claudius Marcellus, vir perfectissimus, rationalis, and Marcius Salutarius, vir egregius, procurator Augustorum, and we ask that the registrar and boundary-fixer be present with you when you come for a perüsonal inspection, and that they make an accurate measurement so that, when the false registration against us has become evident, there may be no controüversy with you over taxes on what he assessed in excess, our right of posüsession thus preserved. Farewell. (3rd H.) I, Valerius Longinus, have subümitted (this petition). (4th H.) I, Valerius Clementinus, have joined in subümitting (this petition). (5th H.) I, Valerius Diogenes, have joined in subümitting (this petition). (2nd H.) Year [.] of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix and Marcus Julius Philippus most noble and renowed Caesar Augusti, Payni [

title: Libellus libellaticorum
provenance: Nartmouthis, Polemonon meris, Arsinoite nome, Egypt
translation_text: To the officials in charge of the sacrifices of the village of Narmouthis, from Aurelius Aunes, son of Silvanus, of the village of Narmouthis. I have always been constant in sacrificing to the gods, and now too, in your presence, in accordance with the regulations, I have sacrified and poured libations and tasted the offerings, and ask you to certify this for me below. May you continue to prosper. I, Aunes, about 19 years old, with a scar on my right elbow.;(2nd hand) I, Aurelius Sarapodorus, have certified. (3rd hand) I, Aurelius Patos, have certified. (4th hand) I, Aurelius NN, have certified (5th hand) I, Aurelius NN, have certified. (6th hand) I, Aurelius NN, have certified. (7th hand) I, Aurelius Itonin, have certified.;(1st hand) Year 1 of the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, Payni 10.

title: List of Names
summary: The papyrus contains a sum, arranged by a daily order, of artabas of wheat received. In fragment A the bookings are indeed made from day to day, but in columns I and II of fragment B certain days are not represented by entries (because the sitologoi didn���t receive anything on these days?)the question arises whether we are dealing here with a private book-keeping of the sitologoi or with an account sent to the strategus.

title: Official Correspondence
summary: In the first place mention is made of the letter written by Pamphilos; next a letter is mentioned, addressed by Apollonios to the strategus Theon in reply to the letter referred to on the recto side; then it is said that a copy of Pamphilos��� letter was attached and finally, that there were copies of the letters sent to Apollonios and Pamphilos.
provenance: Theadelphia, Themistou meris, Arsinoite nome, province of Egypt
translation_text: So far the letter from the person raising objection. Added to this was a reply from Apollonios, the guardian of the banks. To Theon, strategus of the Arsinoite nome, of the merides of Themistes and Polemon, Apollonios, the guardian of the banks, to his most beloved, greetings. Letter. Below was attached the copy of the letter from the personraising objection. So far also the (copies) of that which was sent to Apollonios and that which was sent to the person raising objections. Year 12 of Antoninos Caesar, the lord, the 25th Pachon in Theadelphia of the Thimstes (division).

title: Letter from the Assistant to an Aigialophlax
summary: Ptolemaios, the leaseholder of the plot of land bearing various kinds of brushwood, had up to 7th Hathyr, not received sufficient water for his drymos. Consequently he wrote a letter on this matter to Aelius Heraclitus (the name Aelius would seem to indicate that he or his father was imperial freedman), the assistant of procurator usiacus, to inform him of the situation and to obtain his co-operation to prevail upon the aigialophlax, who is, among other things, charged with the distribution and supply of water, that he will still receive the usual amount of water and secure compensation in case of damage.
provenance: Theadelphia, Themistou meris, Arsinoite nome, province of Egypt
translation_text: Aelius Heraclitus to Apollonius, shore guard, my most beloved, greetings. I had not expected that a petition would reach me concerning the brushwood for you to supply the adequate amount of water as Ptolemaios, the leaseholder thereof has requested me. It fell to your care, did it not, before he requested, to see to this as well. If up to now anything has acted impedingly or anything else of the things which seemed to you to be more urgent, caused delay in filling the brushwood, then see now to it, brother that the usual amount of water is supplied to it. I pray that you are inn good health, my most beloved. Year 8 of Antoninus, the 7th Hathyr.
